title: World's busiest ports, 1550–1815 — decade rankings by three metrics
note: Scholarly best-effort synthesis. Per-decade top-10 by three metrics; order within a decade is indicative, presence is the firmer claim. Anchored to hard regional series where they exist (see sources); estimate elsewhere. Not consumed by the live simulation — the 15-port sim is unchanged. Tier-2 (ranks 11–20) is an UNRANKED second tier per metric per decade (membership, not order); the top-10 remains ranked.

ships.label: Ship movements
ships.basis: Counts hulls, not their size or cargo. Favours dense short-haul and bulk hubs (Baltic grain, North-Sea and Mediterranean coasting); the great long-distance ports carried few but huge ships and stay low. BASIS (R1): counts FOREIGN-GOING clearances/passages, commensurable with the anchoring series (Sound Toll, customs, Carrera registers). Coastal shipping is excluded by construction — which is why Newcastle (whose collier trade was Britain's largest generator of ship movements) ranks under tonnage but not here. That exclusion is a declared silence, carried to the silences register (PLAN-3 R2).

tonnage.label: Tonnage
tonnage.basis: Weights each ship by size. Lifts the large-ship trades — East Indiamen (Batavia, Canton), the Atlantic galleon/Indiaman ports, the English coal fleet (Newcastle) and Baltic bulk (Danzig, Riga) — over small-ship coasting hubs like Genoa, Venice and Marseille.

value.label: Approx. value
value.basis: Weights each cargo by worth. Lifts the high-value commodity ports — American silver (Seville, Cádiz), spices and tea (Lisbon, Amsterdam, Batavia, Canton), sugar/coffee/slaves (Bordeaux, Nantes, Havana) and Bengal (Calcutta) — and drops low-value bulk (Baltic grain and timber, coal).

sources.title: Sound Toll Registers Online (STRO)
sources.author: Veluwenkamp, Scheltjens & van der Woude, TSEG 2021
sources.note: ~1.8 M Øresund passages, ~300 of 360 years 1497–1857; near-complete on passages. Backbone for Baltic/North-Sea ports.

sources.title: Dutch-Asiatic Shipping (DAS)
sources.author: Bruijn, Gaastra & Schöffer, Huygens ING/KNAW
sources.note: >4,700 outbound / >3,400 homeward VOC voyages 1595–1795, each with ship tonnage — anchors Amsterdam & Batavia tonnage.

sources.title: Carrera de Indias (fiscal reinterpretation)
sources.author: Lamikiz, Rev. de Historia Económica / JILAEH (Cambridge) 2024
sources.note: Seville→Cádiz Spanish-Atlantic tonnage fell ~76.9% from the 1610s to the 1690s; 1650 benchmark 39 ships / 8,283 tons — anchors the silver-trade value & tonnage.

sources.title: The Baltic “mother of all trades”
sources.author: van Tielhof (Brill 2002); EH.net; Revue de l'OFCE 2015
sources.note: Amsterdam-centred grain trade in four phases (expansion 1540–1650 → decline 1800–1860); grain is high-volume, low value-per-ton.

sources.title: St Petersburg & Baltic volume
sources.author: Kaukiainen, Int'l Journal of Maritime History 2021 & 2025 (STRO-based)
sources.note: Kronstadt arrivals ~241/yr (1744–48) → ~970/yr (1793–96); a “big port” only by the 1790s, Copenhagen level with it.

sources.title: Bristol & the English outports
sources.author: Bristol Historical Association (Minchinton)
sources.note: Clearances 240 (1687) → 448 (1787); “by 1800 she had already lost her position as the leading outport” to Liverpool.

boundary: DECLARED BOUNDARY (PLAN-3 §1): these rankings order ports within the EUROPEAN COMMERCIAL RECORD — the toll, customs, and company series that survive. Ports whose traffic that record cannot measure are not ranked here regardless of their real size: Istanbul, by any physical measure among the great ports of this whole period, is the exemplar (present, unranked, in the tier-3 promotion queue). Absence from these tables is a statement about the archive, not about the sea.

changelog.decisions: ships basis = foreign-going; promotions: Goa (value T1 1550s-80s), Cap-Français (value T1 1770s-80s), Kingston (value T2 1740s+), Rio de Janeiro (value T1 1740s-60s)
changelog.capped: Porto ships-T2 ends 1750s (slot budget); Rotterdam tonnage-T2 gap 1730s-80s (slot budget); both documented rather than silently absorbed

value 1740s T1: Havana → Rio de Janeiro (Brazilian gold fleets at their height)

value 1760s T1: Havana → Rio de Janeiro (Brazilian gold fleets at their height)

value 1780s T1: Cap-Français ranked above Havana
